CalifOrnia
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
August 2, 
■99■
」ames R. Sutton
NIELSEN′  MERKSAMER′  HODCSON′
PARRINELLO & MUELLER
650 California Street′
 Suite 2650
San Franc■sco′  CA  94108
Re:
Your Request fOr lnfOrmal Ass■
stance
Our File No. I-91-156
Dear Mr. sutton:
This letter is in response to your request for written advice
relating to telephone advice provided to you on February 13, 1991'
unaer tfre new provisions of tire Political Reform Act (the rraggrrll
which Iimit gifts and honoraria. Your request is general in
nature and does not refer to a particular governmental decision
but rather seeks general guidance regarding certain provisions of
law. Accordingly, we treat your Ietter as a request for informal
assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329''
The issues raised in your Ietter present policy questions
which the Cornmission wil,l Lonsider in the near future. 
Our advice
is subject to change as nel', regulations concerning these matters
are adopted.
OUESTIONS
1. Under the provisions of the Act, are travel and
acconmodations provided to a state officiaL in connection with a
speaking event reportable or subject to limits?
1 Gor"..,."nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. AIl statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Comnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
L8ooo, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) .)
428 J Strect,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramcnto CA 95804‐ 0807 0 (916)322・5660


File No. I -91- 156
Page 2
2. Under the provisions of the Act, are travel and
accommodations provlded to a state official 
in connection with a
speaking event but which are not necessary for the state.
oificiai's 
participation in the event reportable and subject to
the t irnits?
3. Under the provisions of the Act, are payments to a state
official 
which are neither directly in connection with a speaking
event nor necessary to the state official's 
participation in the
event gifts to the official?
CONCLUS ION
1. Accommodations and intrastate travel which are Provided
directJ.y in connection uith a speaking event and which are
,,"""...iy 
to the official's 
attendance at the event are neither
reportable nor subject to linits.
2. Travel and accon:nodations which are directly in
connection with a speaking event but rrhich are not necessary to
the official's 
atte;dance at the event are reportable but are not
subject to I i:nits.
3. Payments rrhich are neither directly in connection-with a
speaking ev3nt and which are not necessary to the of f ici'al's
aitendaice at a speaking event are reportable gifts subject to
I imits .
ANALYSI S
Effective January 1, 1991 certain provisions of the Act
a"g..-ing gifts and h-onoraria vere anended. The Act now provides
that:
(a) No elected state officer Day accept an
honorarium.
(b) "Honorariumrr neans, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any palment Eade in consideration
for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, neeting, social event, meal, or Iike
gathering.
Section 89502 (a) and (b).
(a) No elected state officer shall accept
gifts fron any single source in any calendar year
with a total value of nore than thro hundred fifty
dollars ($250).
Section 89504 (a) .


File No. I -91- 15 6
Page 3
The commission wiLl be considering the adoption of
regulations in the near future to clarify and irnplernent the new
stitutes. 
As you correctly Point out, the Cornmission has existing
regulations regarding Lirnitations on gifts and travel. 
Although
th6se regulations predate the current statutes cited above, staff
continuei to provide interim advice in accordance wj'th these
regulations. For exanple, Regulation 18728 provides as follows:
....free adnission, food, beverages and
sirnilar norninal benefits provided to a filer at an
event at which he or she speaks, participates in a
panel or seninar or performs a sinilar service, and
ieinburseruent or advance for actual intrastate
travel and for necessarv accommodations provided
directly in connection with the event are not
paynents and need not be reported by the filer.
Regulation 18728(a) , emphasis added.
Regulation 18623(a) provides a sinj'Iar exception.
As we discussed during the course of our telephone
conversation, and as you correctly sunmarize the advice in your
request for advice, the statutory scheme specifically prohibits
stite officials 
from receivi.ng honoraria. (Section 895o2')
However, reimbursement or payment for actual travel expenses and
reason"ble subsistence provided directty in connection with a
speech gi.ven is not prohibited. 
we have previously advised that
tire teri 
r accommodat-ions" includes lodging and meaIs. (See PuqIi.a
Advice Letter, No. A-7?-464; chel Advice Letter, No. A-78-264;
Tebbetts Advice Letter, No. I-91-186.)
The extent to lrhich food and lodging are 'rnecessaryrt in
connection with speaking at an event nust be considered on a case-
by-case basis. 
Pa)rments for necessary acconnodations are not
disclosable and do not trigger a conflict of interest' 
Thus,
where intrastate travel and accouunodations are provided in
connection with a speech or with participation in a panel or
seminar consistent with Regulation L8728, the travel and
accomrnodations need not be reported and are not subject to the
gift and honoraria limits or the conf l ict-of - interest provisions
of the Act.
The extent to which food and lodging are rrnecessary" in
connection with a speaking event nust be considered on a case-by-
case basis. 
(Tebbetts Advice Letter, supra. ) Any accommodations
or other nominal benefits provided to a state official 
rrhich are
not necessary or directty in connection uith the official's
attendance at the gathering for purposes of speaking to the
assenbly may be gifts and not covered by this very limited
exception.


FiIe No. I -91- 156
Page 4
we also concur r.rith your determination that, under Section
89506(a), travel and acco:unodations r.rhich are directLy in
connection with a speaking event are not prohibited or subject to
limits as follows:
(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, inctuding actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or Iirnited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:
(1) The travel is in connection with a speech
given by the elected state officer, nember of a
state board or conraission, or designated employee
of a state agency, the lodging and subsistence
expenses are limited to the day inmediately
preceding, the day of, and the day immed j'ately
iollowing the speech, and the travel is within the
United States.
(2) The travel, is provided by a government, a
governmental agency, a foreign government, a
governrnental authority, a bona fide public or
private educational institution, 
as defined in
section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, or a
nonprofit charitable or reJ.igious organization
which is exempt from taxation under Section
501(c) (3) of the InternaL Revenue code, or by a
person domiciled outside the United states lrhich
substantially satisfies the requirements for tax
exempt status under Section 501(c) (3) of the
fnternal Revenue code.
section 89 506 (a) .
Thus, $/hile travel pa)rments which are not exempted by Regrulation
18728 are reportable, such paytnents are not linited if they fa1l
under one of the two categories indicated in Section 89506(a).
In addition, other benefits received by a state official 
in
connection with a speech, such as admission to social gatherings
held in connection r./ith the event, are reportable gifts. 
Section
89504 linits gifts to state officials. 
A state official 
nay not
accept gifts from any singJ.e source in any calendar year in excess
of S25o. Moreover, any gift in excess of $50 from a single source
is reportab.l,e and gifts of $250 subject the state official 
to
disqua l ification under the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the
Act. 
We have previously advised that greens fees, mea1s,
refreshments, transportation, and entertainrnent provided to
elected state officers and state adninistrative officials 
in


File No. I -91- 155
Page 5
connection with attendance at an event and provided by the event
sponsors are reportable gifts subject to the limits. 
(Lowell
advice Letter, No. I-91-2o8. ) SiEilarIy, tickets to entertainment
activities or palnnents for lodging which are in excess of what is
minimally required for the recipient to make a presentation at a
conference are reportable gifts subject to limits and are not
prohibited honoraria.
we trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (915) 322-59oL.5
s incere ly,
scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
a) __

a-fi:- Etanca Y!- Breeze /
counsel, Legal Division
SH:BMB
' 
copies of Commission regulations and opinions are available in
nany law libraries. 
Alternatively, copies of these naterials and
Commission advice Ietters may be obtained from the Cornmission at a
cost of 10C per page.
